revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:晡時臥山]]
'''Kurefushi no Yama''' (晡時臥山) is a {{ill|Mountain|en|Mountain|qid=Q8502|lt=mountain|1=Mountain}} mentioned in the {{ill|Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|ja|常陸国風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|qq=}}, under the entry for Naka District. Several {{ill|Folklore|ja|民話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Folklore|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Folktale|qid=Q1221280}} and legends about this mountain have been passed down. It is believed to be present-day {{ill|Asabōyama|ja|朝房山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asabōyama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which spans {{ill|Mito, Ibaraki|en|Mito, Ibaraki|qid=Q204249|lt=Mito|1=Mito, Ibaraki}}, {{ill|Kasama, Ibaraki|en|Kasama, Ibaraki|qid=Q845851|lt=Kasama|1=Kasama, Ibaraki}}, and {{ill|Shirosato, Ibaraki|en|Shirosato, Ibaraki|qid=Q1203068|lt=Shirosato, Ibaraki|1=Shirosato, Ibaraki}} in {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|qid=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}.
== Location == Kurefushi no Yama is presumed to be {{ill|Asabōyama|ja|朝房山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asabōyama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, spanning modern-day {{ill|Mito, Ibaraki|en|Mito, Ibaraki|qid=Q204249|lt=Mito|1=Mito, Ibaraki}}, {{ill|Kasama, Ibaraki|en|Kasama, Ibaraki|qid=Q845851|lt=Kasama|1=Kasama, Ibaraki}}, and {{ill|Shirosato, Ibaraki|en|Shirosato, Ibaraki|qid=Q1203068|lt=Shirosato, Ibaraki|1=Shirosato, Ibaraki}}.<ref name="kurefu">{{Cite web|title=Kurefushi Yama "Asabōyama"|url=http://www.bunkajoho.pref.ibaraki.jp/fudoki/visit/20/index.html|publisher=Ibaraki Prefecture Cultural Affairs Division|accessdate=2017-11-24}}</ref> In the {{ill|Shinpen Hitachi Koku-shi|ja|新編常陸国誌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpen Hitachi Koku-shi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, compiled by the Hitachi scholar {{ill|Nobuna Nakayama|ja|中山信名|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nobuna Nakayama|1=Nakayama Nobuna|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q128843419}}, Kurefushi no Yama is described as being located north of Ushibuse Village in Ibaraki District: "North of Ibaraki District's Ushibuse Village... now called Asabōyama. Ushibuse corresponds to Kurefushi."<ref>{{NDLJP|763973}}, Nakayama Nobuna, ''Shinpen Hitachi Koku-shi,'' Volume 6, Yamakawa, p. 1090</ref>
== Legends == === Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|ja|常陸国風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|qq=}} records the following legend about Kurefushi no Yama:
: On a high hill north of Ibaraki no Sato, there was a mountain called Kurefushi no Yama, where a brother and sister, Nukabiko and Nukabime, lived. A mysterious suitor appeared every night at Nukabime’s home. When she accepted his proposal, she conceived in one night and eventually gave birth to a small snake. This snake could speak with its mother during the night but remained silent during the day. Shocked and believing the snake to be a divine child, Nukabime placed it in a purified cup and offered it on an altar. The snake grew large enough to fill the cup in a single night. They replaced it with larger vessels repeatedly, but the snake always grew to fill them. Eventually, they ran out of suitable vessels. Nukabime urged the snake to leave for its father’s home, as she could no longer care for it. Though the snake was sorrowful, it requested a companion for its journey. Nukabime explained there were no other people to accompany it. Angered, the snake killed Nukabiko and attempted to ascend to the heavens. Nukabime threw a basin at the snake, causing it to fall and remain on the mountain. The vessel used for the snake still exists in Kataoka Village.
The original text in Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki is as follows: {{Quotation| 茨城里自此以北高丘名曰晡時臥之山古老曰有兄妹二人兄名努賀毗古妹名努賀毗咩...其子孫立社致祭相續不絶 |Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|Naka District}}
This story shares similarities with other legends, such as the {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|qid=Q860627|lt=Miwa Mountain Legend|1=Mount Miwa}} in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|qid=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}, the {{ill|Kamo Legend|ja|賀茂伝承|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Legend|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} from the Yamashiro no Kuni Fudoki fragments,<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%99%A1%E6%99%82%E8%87%A5%E5%B1%B1%E4%BC%9D%E8%AA%AC-1307364|title=Kurefushi no Yama Legend|publisher=World Encyclopedia 2nd Edition - Kotobank|accessdate=2017-11-24}}</ref> and the story of a nightly visiting snake in the {{ill|Hizen no Kuni Fudoki|ja|肥前国風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hizen no Kuni Fudoki|qq=}}.<ref name="ibabun"/>
=== Daidarabō === In Ōashi, Mito City, Kurefushi no Yama is also associated with {{ill|Daidarabō|ja|ダイダラボウ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daidarabō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ダイダラボッチ|1=Daidarabotchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1157529}} legends.<ref name="ibabun">{{Cite web|author=Imase Fumiya|title=Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki and Folktales|url=http://ibabun.com/hitachi.pdf|format=PDF|publisher=Ibaraki Prefecture Folk Culture Promotion Foundation|accessdate=2017-11-24}}</ref> According to one tale, the mountain's original location to the southwest caused shadows that shortened daylight. To resolve this, Daidarabō moved the mountain. The name "Kurefushi" is said to mean "preventing sunset."<ref name="ibabun"/>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Fudoki (Vol. 1)|author=Nakamura Keishin|year=2015|series=Kadokawa Sophia Bunko|publisher=Kadokawa Gakugei Publishing|isbn=978-4044001193}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|ja|常陸国風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|qq=}}
